## claude.ai Amplitude
Amplitude is a product analytics platform. Most tools require a projectId (appId).

At the start of a session, call 'get_context' to see the user's organization and accessible projects. For detailed project settings, use 'get_project_context'.

Event and property names are project-specific — use 'get_events' or 'get_event_properties' to discover valid names before using them in filters. Never assume or guess event names.

When answering data questions, prefer visual answers. Use 'query_chart' to deep-dive into a single chart — it renders an interactive chart in the client. Use 'query_charts' only when comparing data across multiple charts. To persist an analysis, use 'create_chart' to save a new chart or add it to a dashboard so users can revisit and share it.

input.prompt:
I need to understand how this Coderegon Trail project indexes external repos for trail games. 

1. Read the memory file at /Users/flowclub/.claude/projects/-Users-flowclub-code-coderegon-trail/memory/trail_game_repos.md to see what repos are already indexed
2. Look at the trail-data-extractor agent (agents/trail-data-extractor.md) to understand how it analyzes repos
3. Look at skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md to understand the trail data format
4. Check the index or any listing of available trails (look for files that list/reference the available games)

Report: What repos are indexed, what data format is used for each trail, and what's needed to add a new repo. Be thorough - include file paths and key structures.

Research DeepWiki.com to understand what it offers and whether its data could be useful for understanding GitHub repos programmatically.

1. Fetch https://deepwiki.com and see what the site is about
2. Try fetching https://deepwiki.com/api or similar API endpoints to see if there's a public API
3. Try fetching a specific repo page like https://deepwiki.com/vercel/next.js to see what kind of data/analysis it provides
4. Check if there's any documented API or data export capability

Report: What DeepWiki does, what data it exposes, whether it has an API, and whether its analysis could be consumed programmatically to understand a repo's architecture and request pipeline. Be specific about what you found vs what returned errors.

Fetch the top Show HN posts that link to GitHub repos from HN Algolia API. The user provided this URL as a starting point:

https://hn.algolia.com/?dateEnd=1775677071&dateRange=custom&dateStart=1767225600&page=0&prefix=true&query=github.com&sort=byPopularity&type=show_hn

The API endpoint format is: https://hn.algolia.com/api/v1/search?query=github.com&tags=show_hn&hitsPerPage=50&numericFilters=created_at_i>1767225600,created_at_i<1775677071

Fetch this API endpoint (and page 1 too if needed) and compile a list of the top Show HN posts with GitHub repos. For each, extract:
- Title
- GitHub repo URL (from the URL field or story text)
- Points (upvotes)
- Number of comments
- Date posted

Sort by points descending. Return the top 30+ results so we can filter further.

Also note: the date range in the URL seems to be from approximately Jan 2026 to some future date. Convert the timestamps to human-readable dates and note the range.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile a comprehensive report: ## Comprehensive Report: Coderegon Trail Project Indexing Based on my analysis of the Coderegon Trail project, here's how external repos are indexed and integrated for trail games: --- ### 1. INDEXED REPOSITORIES (7 External + 6 Framework Built-ins) The memory file at `/Users/flowclub/.claude/projects/-Users-flowclub-code-coderegon-trail/memory/trail_game_repos.md` maps these external repos: | Trail Name | Directory | GitHub Repo | |---|---|---| | The Gateway Trail | openclaw/ | https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw | | The Hybrid Search Trail | qmd/ | tobi/qmd | | The Agent Loop Trail | pi-mono/ | badlogic/pi-mono | | The WiFi Sensing Trail | ruview/ | ruvnet/RuView | | The Exploit Trail | shannon/ | KeygraphHQ/shannon | | The Spacetime Trail | spacetimedb/ | clockworklabs/SpacetimeDB | | The Superpowers Trail | superpowers/ | obra/superpowers | Plus 6 canonical framework trails (Next.js, Rails, Django, Express, React/Vite, Laravel) + gstack = 13 total games. --- ### 2. TRAIL DATA FORMAT Each trail is stored as a self-contained `{game}/index.html` file containing embedded JSON at `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/{game}/index.html`. **Core Structure:** ```javascript window.TRAIL_DATA = { "framework": "openclaw", // unique identifier "trailName": "The Gateway Trail", // display name "stops": [ /* 8 stops maximum */ ], // pipeline stages "events": [ /* 15-20 events */ ], // quiz + fortune events "partyMembers": [ /* 4 members */ ], // framework concepts "deathMessages": [ /* array */ ], // death flavor text "sourceInfo": { // tracking metadata "repo": "openclaw/openclaw", "commit": "f3ecd9c", "tag": "v2026.3.24", "snapshotDate": "2026-03-28" } } ``` **Stop Structure (each of ~8):** ```javascript { "name": "Channel Outpost", // stop landmark name "subtitle": "Message Arrives", // stage description "concept": "Channel Plugins", // framework concept tested "landmarkType": "town|camp|mountain|river|forest", // visual asset "code": { "file": "extensions/whatsapp/src/channel.ts", "content": "// actual code snippet (15-25 lines)", "language": "typescript|ruby|python|javascript", "startLine": 1, "endLine": 18 }, "narration": "Conversational TTS-friendly text (3-5 sentences, <25 words each)" } ``` **Event Structure (quiz or fortune):** ```javascript { "type": "weather|river|encounter|misfortune|fortune", "trigger": "after_stop", "triggerStop": 1, // which stop triggers it "title": "Protocol Storm!", "text": "Scenario description", "choices": [ { "text": "Option A", "correct": true, "explanation": "Why this is right" }, // ... 2 more choices (or 0 for fortune events) ], "concept": "Channel Plugins", // party member concept "difficulty": "easy|medium|hard" } ``` **Party Members (4 per framework):** ```javascript { "name": "Gateway Control Plane", "icon": "gateway|channel|route|session", "maxHealth": 3 } ``` --- ### 3. DATA EXTRACTION PIPELINE Located at `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/agents/trail-data-extractor.md`: **Three Extraction Modes:** 1. **Canonical Mode** (Framework name given) - Reads `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md` - Contains pre-built trails for: Next.js, Rails, Django, Express, React/Vite, Laravel, OpenClaw - Lines 7-2800+: Full trail definitions with stops, events, party members 2. **Repo Mode** (External repository analysis) - Detects framework from telltale files: - `next.config.js`/`next.config.mjs` → Next.js - `Gemfile` with `rails` → Rails - `manage.py` + `settings.py` → Django - `package.json` with `express` → Express - `package.json` with `react` + `vite.config` → React/Vite - `composer.json` with `laravel/framework` → Laravel - Maps pipeline stages to actual files - Extracts 15-25 line code snippets from each stage - Generates TTS-friendly narration per snippet - Creates 4-6 quiz events 3. **PR Mode** (Diff analysis for PR trails) - Located at `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/agents/pr-trail-extractor.md` - Analyzes diffs instead of full repos - Creates themed "PR trails" with destination "Merge Frontier" --- ### 4. TRAIL GENERATION WORKFLOW **File Location:** `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/commands/fly-visual.md` **Generation Steps:** 1. Parse user input: - Framework name → use canonical trail - PR number/URL/git-range → spawn pr-trail-extractor agent - Empty/repo context → auto-detect with trail-data-extractor agent 2. Extract TRAIL_DATA: - Canonical: Read from `framework-trails.md` - Custom: Spawn trail-data-extractor agent - Fetch JSON from agent or reference file 3. Generate HTML: - Use `frontend-design` skill - Embed TRAIL_DATA as inline JavaScript - Include per-game customization: - `window.TRAIL_FLAVORS` — flavor text array - `window.TRAIL_CONFIG` — mountain colors, styling - `window.drawCustomEventOverlay(time)` — event animations 4. Load shared engine: - All games load `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/engine.js` (68KB) - Contains: CSS, canvas rendering, state machine, audio, Shiki syntax highlighting, UI/input 5. Open in browser via `/open-viz.sh` --- ### 5. GAME LISTING & INDEX **Main Index:** `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/index.html` - Three.js 3D disk visualization - Lists all 13 trails with HUD navigation - Fallback: HTML list of trail links - Trails array in JavaScript (line ~83): ```javascript const TRAILS = [ { name: "The Gateway Trail", subtitle: "OpenClaw", href: "openclaw/", color: "#8B95A2" }, { name: "The Convention Trail", subtitle: "Ruby on Rails", href: "rails/", color: "#CC0000" }, // ... 11 more ]; ``` **Game Discovery Script:** `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/scripts/lib/trail-data.js` - `findGames()` - scans root for directories with `index.html` containing `window.TRAIL_DATA` - `extractTrailData(htmlPath)` - parses JSON from HTML - `loadAllGames()` - returns all indexed games + errors **Auditing Tool:** `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/scripts/audit-trails.js` - Validates all trail data - Reports: framework, stops, events, party members, staleness - Filters by age: `--stale 30` shows games >30 days old --- ### 6. ADDING A NEW REPO TO THE PROJECT **Step-by-Step Process:** 1. **Create game directory:** ```bash mkdir /Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/{new-game}/ ``` 2. **Generate trail data** (one of two methods): **Option A: Canonical Framework** - If it's an existing framework (Rails, Django, etc.): - Use `/fly-visual rails` → automatically reads framework-trails.md - No new data needed **Option B: External Repository** - Run `/fly-visual` with repo name or auto-detect - Spawn trail-data-extractor agent (agents/trail-data-extractor.md) - Agent analyzes repo, returns TRAIL_DATA JSON block with `sourceInfo` metadata - Agent extracts: - 8 stops matching framework pipeline - 15-25 line code snippets from actual files - TTS narration (conversational, <25 words/sentence) - 4-6 quiz events (easy/medium/hard difficulties) - 4 party members (framework concepts) 3. **Create index.html:** ```html
...
``` 4. **Update memory file:** - Add entry to `/Users/flowclub/.claude/projects/-Users-flowclub-code-coderegon-trail/memory/trail_game_repos.md` - Format: `| Trail Name | directory/ | github-repo-link |` 5. **Update main index:** - Add entry to TRAILS array in `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/index.html` (line ~83) - Add fallback link in HTML (line ~114) 6. **Optional: Add custom event overlay** - Implement `window.drawCustomEventOverlay(time)` for unique event animations - Example: OpenClaw has custom "Protocol Storm" lightning effects --- ### 7. KEY FILES & PATHS | File | Purpose | |------|---------| | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/engine.js` | Shared game engine (68KB) | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/index.html` | Main game selector page | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/{game}/index.html` | Per-game HTML (3-50KB depending on trail data size) | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md` | Canonical trail definitions (3141 lines) | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/agents/trail-data-extractor.md` | Framework detection + repo analysis agent | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/agents/pr-trail-extractor.md` | PR diff analysis agent | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/commands/fly-visual.md` | Game generation + launching command | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/scripts/lib/trail-data.js` | Game discovery + JSON extraction | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/scripts/audit-trails.js` | Trail validation & reporting | | `/Users/flowclub/.claude/projects/...memory/trail_game_repos.md` | External repo mapping (11 days old) | --- ### 8. NARRATION REQUIREMENTS All narration must be: - **Conversational:** "Let's look at...", "Notice how...", "The reason for this is..." - **TTS-friendly:** Sentences <25 words, spell abbreviations, say "slash" for paths - **Explanatory:** Focus on WHY, not WHAT (never read code syntax literally) - **Progressive:** Overview first, details on demand Example (OpenClaw): > "Every journey starts at a channel outpost. OpenClaw connects to twenty different messaging channels, from WhatsApp to Discord to Matrix. Each uses createChatChannelPlugin from the plugin SDK. Here the WhatsApp channel wires up outbound messaging with text chunking and target resolution, all following the same plugin interface." --- ### 9. TRAIL STALENESS TRACKING The `sourceInfo` object tracks each external repo's currency: ```javascript "sourceInfo": { "repo": "openclaw/openclaw", "commit": "f3ecd9c", "tag": "v2026.3.24", "snapshotDate": "2026-03-28" } ``` Use `scripts/audit-trails.js --stale 30` to find games older than 30 days. The memory file's timestamp indicates when repo mappings were last verified. --- This architecture allows the project to scale: new repos can be indexed by running the trail-data-extractor agent against any GitHub repo, and the framework detection is flexible enough to handle custom setups with lower confidence.

``` 4. **Update memory file:** - Add entry to `/Users/flowclub/.claude/projects/-Users-flowclub-code-coderegon-trail/memory/trail_game_repos.md` - Format: `| Trail Name | directory/ | github-repo-link |` 5. **Update main index:** - Add entry to TRAILS array in `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/index.html` (line ~83) - Add fallback link in HTML (line ~114) 6. **Optional: Add custom event overlay** - Implement `window.drawCustomEventOverlay(time)` for unique event animations - Example: OpenClaw has custom "Protocol Storm" lightning effects --- ### 7. KEY FILES & PATHS | File | Purpose | |------|---------| | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/engine.js` | Shared game engine (68KB) | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/index.html` | Main game selector page | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/{game}/index.html` | Per-game HTML (3-50KB depending on trail data size) | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md` | Canonical trail definitions (3141 lines) | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/agents/trail-data-extractor.md` | Framework detection + repo analysis agent | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/agents/pr-trail-extractor.md` | PR diff analysis agent | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/commands/fly-visual.md` | Game generation + launching command | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/scripts/lib/trail-data.js` | Game discovery + JSON extraction | | `/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/scripts/audit-trails.js` | Trail validation & reporting | | `/Users/flowclub/.claude/projects/...memory/trail_game_repos.md` | External repo mapping (11 days old) | --- ### 8. NARRATION REQUIREMENTS All narration must be: - **Conversational:** "Let's look at...", "Notice how...", "The reason for this is..." - **TTS-friendly:** Sentences <25 words, spell abbreviations, say "slash" for paths - **Explanatory:** Focus on WHY, not WHAT (never read code syntax literally) - **Progressive:** Overview first, details on demand Example (OpenClaw): > "Every journey starts at a channel outpost. OpenClaw connects to twenty different messaging channels, from WhatsApp to Discord to Matrix. Each uses createChatChannelPlugin from the plugin SDK. Here the WhatsApp channel wires up outbound messaging with text chunking and target resolution, all following the same plugin interface." --- ### 9. TRAIL STALENESS TRACKING The `sourceInfo` object tracks each external repo's currency: ```javascript "sourceInfo": { "repo": "openclaw/openclaw", "commit": "f3ecd9c", "tag": "v2026.3.24", "snapshotDate": "2026-03-28" } ``` Use `scripts/audit-trails.js --stale 30` to find games older than 30 days. The memory file's timestamp indicates when repo mappings were last verified. --- This architecture allows the project to scale: new repos can be indexed by running the trail-data-extractor agent against any GitHub repo, and the framework detection is flexible enough to handle custom setups with lower confidence.

Now I have a thorough picture. Let me compile my findings.

---

## DeepWiki.com Research Report

### What DeepWiki Is

DeepWiki (built by Cognition, the company behind Devin) is an AI-generated documentation platform for GitHub repositories. Its tagline is "up-to-date documentation you can talk to, for every repo in the world." It automatically indexes public GitHub repos and generates structured, multi-page technical wikis with architectural analysis, Mermaid diagrams, source code references, and tables.

**URL pattern:** Replace `github.com` with `deepwiki.com` -- e.g., `https://deepwiki.com/expressjs/express` produces a full wiki for Express.js. Individual pages are accessible at paths like `/expressjs/express/2.4-request-response-lifecycle`.

### What Data It Provides

For each indexed repo, DeepWiki generates:

| Content Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Hierarchical wiki structure** | 8-10 top-level sections with subsections (e.g., "Core Architecture > Request-Response Lifecycle") |
| **Architectural narratives** | Multi-paragraph explanations of how subsystems work, with design rationale |
| **Mermaid diagrams** | Sequence diagrams, dependency graphs, flow charts embedded in each page |
| **Source code references** | Inline citations like `[lib/application.js:152-178]()` linking to specific file:line ranges at the indexed commit |
| **Tables** | Component mappings, dependency matrices, comparison tables |
| **JSON-LD metadata** | Schema.org `TechArticle` structured data on every page |
| **Commit-pinned snapshots** | Each wiki notes its indexed commit hash and date |

Notably, it does **not** index GitHub Issues, PRs, or commit history -- only the source code at a point-in-time snapshot.

### Programmatic Access -- The MCP Server

DeepWiki exposes a **free, no-authentication-required MCP (Model Context Protocol) server** for public repos.

**Endpoint:** `https://mcp.deepwiki.com/mcp` (Streamable HTTP, recommended)  
**Legacy SSE endpoint:** `https://mcp.deepwiki.com/sse` (being deprecated)

**Three public tools available (verified by calling `tools/list`):**

| Tool | Parameters | What It Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `read_wiki_structure` | `repoName` (string, e.g. `"rails/rails"`) | Hierarchical table of contents -- numbered sections and subsections |
| `read_wiki_contents` | `repoName` (string) | **Full wiki content** as markdown -- all pages concatenated with Mermaid diagrams, source references, tables. Can be very large (1.2MB for Express.js) |
| `ask_question` | `repoName` (string or array of up to 10), `question` (string) | AI-powered Q&A grounded in the wiki. **However, this tool returned "Repository not found" for repos I tested (expressjs/express, vercel/next.js) even though their wikis exist.** May require authentication or specific indexing state. |

**Private repo access** requires a Devin API key at a different endpoint: `https://mcp.devin.ai/mcp` with an `Authorization: Bearer` header. This also unlocks additional tools: `list_available_repos`, `devin_knowledge_manage`, `devin_session_create`, and others.

**No REST API was found.** The `/api` path just returns the HTML homepage. There are no documented JSON API endpoints outside of MCP.

### Key Findings for Coderegon Trail Use Case

**High relevance for understanding request pipelines:**
- DeepWiki's wiki structure for frameworks maps closely to what the trail-data-extractor agent needs. For example, Rails has a dedicated "1.2 Request-Response Cycle" page, Express has "2.4 Request-Response Lifecycle" and "4.1 Middleware Execution Flow", Django has "Core Django ORM" sections, and Laravel has "2.1 HTTP Request Processing".
- The `read_wiki_structure` tool reliably returns a clean table of contents that could help identify which pipeline stages a framework has.
- The `read_wiki_contents` tool returns full markdown with Mermaid diagrams and file:line references -- exactly the kind of structured architectural data that could feed trail generation.

**Practical limitations:**
- `read_wiki_contents` dumps the entire wiki (~1.2MB for Express, likely larger for Rails/Next.js). There is no page-specific parameter (I tested passing `page` and got a Pydantic validation error). This makes it unwieldy for targeted queries.
- `ask_question` -- the most useful tool for targeted queries -- returned errors for repos I tested. It may require authentication or may not work for all repos via the public endpoint.
- The data is a point-in-time snapshot, not live. Freshness depends on when DeepWiki last re-indexed.
- No rate limits are documented, but none are guaranteed either.

**Integration approach that would work today:** Use `read_wiki_structure` to get the TOC, then use `read_wiki_contents` and parse the markdown to extract specific sections about request pipelines. The structured section numbering (e.g., "2.4-request-response-lifecycle") is consistent and parseable.

Sources:
- [DeepWiki Developer Guide 2026](https://ghost.codersera.com/blog/deepwiki-complete-developer-guide-2026/)
- [DeepWiki MCP Server - Cognition Blog](https://cognition.ai/blog/deepwiki-mcp-server)
- [DeepWiki MCP - Devin Docs](https://docs.devin.ai/work-with-devin/deepwiki-mcp)
- [DeepWiki MCP Server - Apidog](https://apidog.com/blog/deepwiki-mcp/)

input.content:
# Plan: Index 10 New Repos from Top Show HN Projects

## Context

We have 13 trail games (7 external repos + 6 canonical frameworks). The user wants to scale by finding 10 more high-quality repos to index. Two angles investigated: (1) using DeepWiki.com to accelerate repo analysis, (2) mining Show HN for popular GitHub projects.

---

## DeepWiki as an Accelerator

**Verdict: Yes, it can help — as a supplement to trail-data-extractor, not a replacement.**

DeepWiki (by Cognition/Devin) auto-generates architectural wikis for any public GitHub repo. Key findings:

| Capability | Useful? | Details |
|---|---|---|
| **MCP Server** (free, no auth) | Yes | `https://mcp.deepwiki.com/mcp` — Streamable HTTP |
| `read_wiki_structure` | Yes | Returns hierarchical TOC — helps identify pipeline stages |
| `read_wiki_contents` | Partially | Full wiki as markdown with diagrams + file:line refs. BUT dumps entire wiki (~1.2MB for Express), no page-specific queries |
| `ask_question` | Broken? | Returned "Repository not found" for tested repos |
| Source references | Yes | Inline `[file.ts:152-178]` citations — maps to trail stop code snippets |
| Pipeline docs | Yes | Generates "Request-Response Lifecycle", "Middleware Flow" etc. sections — directly maps to trail stops |

**How to integrate:** Before running trail-data-extractor on a new repo, fetch `read_wiki_structure` to identify the pipeline stages, then pass that context to the extractor agent. This gives the agent a head start on understanding the architecture without reading every file.

**Limitation:** The full wiki dump is too large to pass directly. Would need to parse the markdown and extract relevant pipeline sections. Could add a lightweight pre-processing step.

---

## Top Show HN Repos — Weighted Ranking

**Source:** HN Algolia API, Jan 1 – Apr 8, 2026, Show HN posts linking to GitHub repos.
**Formula:** `score = upvotes × 3 + stars × 0.1` (upvotes weighted ~30x per unit)

| Rank | Score | Repo | Upvotes | Stars | Language | Pipeline Type |
|------|-------|------|---------|-------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | 3031 | [KittenML/KittenTTS](https://github.com/KittenML/KittenTTS) | 561 | 13.5K | Python | TTS: text → normalize → phoneme → acoustic model → vocoder → audio |
| 2 | 1916 | [manaflow-ai/cmux](https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux) | 198 | 13.2K | Swift? | Terminal: input → tab mgmt → PTY → render → notifications |
| 3 | 1841 | [puemos/craftplan](https://github.com/puemos/craftplan) | 577 | 1.1K | Web | Production mgmt: request → auth → scheduling → inventory → response |
| 4 | 1631 | [rowboatlabs/rowboat](https://github.com/rowboatlabs/rowboat) | 205 | 10.2K | Python/TS | AI agent: input → entity extraction → graph → context → LLM → response |
| 5 | 1277 | [htdt/godogen](https://github.com/htdt/godogen) | 337 | 2.7K | GDScript | Game gen: prompt → scene → nodes → scripts → project assembly |
| 6 | 1103 | [localgpt-app/localgpt](https://github.com/localgpt-app/localgpt) | 331 | 1.1K | Rust | Local AI: request → memory → context → inference → response |
| 7 | 1006 | [valdanylchuk/breezydemo](https://github.com/valdanylchuk/breezydemo) | 327 | 247 | C/Embedded | ESP32: boot → shell → input → command parse → execute → display |
| 8 | 993 | [generalaction/emdash](https://github.com/generalaction/emdash) | 206 | 3.7K | TS | Dev env: file watch → parse → agent loop → edit → validate |
| 9 | 971 | [timescale/pg_textsearch](https://github.com/timescale/pg_textsearch) | 203 | 3.6K | Rust/C | Search: query → tokenize → BM25 score → index scan → rank → results |
| 10 | 891 | [19-84/redd-archiver](https://github.com/19-84/redd-archiver) | 286 | 330 | ? | Archive: fetch → parse → store → index → serve |
| 11 | 881 | [moongate-community/moongatev2](https://github.com/moongate-community/moongatev2) | 287 | 199 | C#/.NET | Game server: connect → packet → state → simulate → response |
| 12 | 864 | [OlaProeis/Ferrite](https://github.com/OlaProeis/Ferrite) | 241 | 1.4K | Rust | Editor: input → parse → AST → render → Mermaid diagrams |
| 13 | 836 | [IronsideXXVI/Hacker-News](https://github.com/IronsideXXVI/Hacker-News) | 265 | 409 | Swift | Mobile: API → JSON parse → SwiftUI state → view render |
| 14 | 710 | [odvcencio/gotreesitter](https://github.com/odvcencio/gotreesitter) | 222 | 443 | Go | Parser: source → lexer → parse → tree → query |
| 15 | 653 | [onecli/onecli](https://github.com/onecli/onecli) | 161 | 1.7K | Rust | Vault: request → auth → secret mgmt → agent context |
| 16 | 635 | [yannick-cw/korb](https://github.com/yannick-cw/korb) | 204 | 225 | Haskell | CLI: auth → reverse-eng API → cart → checkout → order |
| 17 | 456 | [beginner-jhj/mini_browser](https://github.com/beginner-jhj/mini_browser) | 146 | 177 | C++ | Browser: HTML → DOM → CSS → layout → paint → render |

### Recommended 10 (optimizing for pipeline clarity + domain diversity)

Excluded domains already covered: gateway/API (OpenClaw), search (qmd), agent loop (pi-mono), database (SpacetimeDB).

| # | Repo | Domain | Why it's a good trail |
|---|------|--------|----------------------|
| 1 | **KittenTTS** | ML/TTS | Clear ML pipeline, massive traction, unique domain |
| 2 | **Craftplan** | Production mgmt | Highest upvotes, web app with scheduling pipeline |
| 3 | **LocalGPT** | Local AI (Rust) | Rust inference pipeline, persistent memory adds depth |
| 4 | **pg_textsearch** | Postgres extension | BM25 scoring pipeline, Rust extension for Postgres — very teachable |
| 5 | **Moongate** | Game server | .NET game server pipeline, packet parsing → world sim — unique domain |
| 6 | **Ferrite** | Text editor (Rust) | Markdown → AST → render pipeline, Mermaid rendering |
| 7 | **gotreesitter** | Compiler/parser (Go) | Classic lexer → parser → tree pipeline, educational |
| 8 | **mini_browser** | Browser engine (C++) | HTML → DOM → CSS → layout → paint — the canonical pipeline |
| 9 | **korb** | CLI (Haskell) | Reverse-engineered API, Haskell is unique, fun domain |
| 10 | **cmux** | Terminal (Swift) | Huge stars, terminal multiplexer pipeline |

**Dropped from top-10 by score:**
- **Rowboat** (score 1631) — overlaps with existing agent/knowledge-graph trails
- **godogen** (score 1277) — Claude Code plugin, too meta/similar to this project
- **emdash** (score 993) — overlaps with agent/dev-env domain
- **breezydemo** (score 1006) — embedded/ESP32 may be hard to trace as a web-style pipeline
- **redd-archiver** (score 891) — simpler fetch/store pipeline, less teachable depth

---

## Implementation Approach

For each of the 10 repos:

1. **Pre-analyze with DeepWiki** (optional accelerator)
   - Call `read_wiki_structure` via MCP to get architectural TOC
   - Identify pipeline-relevant sections
   
2. **Run trail-data-extractor agent** against the repo
   - Pass DeepWiki context if available
   - Agent detects framework, maps pipeline to ~8 stops, extracts code + narration
   
3. **Generate game HTML**
   - Create `{game}/index.html` with embedded TRAIL_DATA
   - Add custom TRAIL_FLAVORS and TRAIL_CONFIG
   
4. **Update index**
   - Add to TRAILS array in `index.html`
   - Update memory file with repo mapping

---

## Verification

- Run `scripts/audit-trails.js` to validate all new trail data
- Open each game in browser to verify playability
- Check that index.html lists all 23 trails (13 existing + 10 new)

---

## Open Questions

1. Should we integrate DeepWiki MCP into the trail-data-extractor agent prompt, or keep it as a manual pre-step?
2. Any repos in the recommended 10 you'd swap out? (e.g., swap korb for Rowboat?)
3. Should we batch all 10, or start with the top 3-5 and iterate?

**How to integrate:** Before running trail-data-extractor on a new repo, fetch `read_wiki_structure` to identify the pipeline stages, then pass that context to the extractor agent. This gives the agent a head start on understanding the architecture without reading every file.

**Limitation:** The full wiki dump is too large to pass directly. Would need to parse the markdown and extract relevant pipeline sections. Could add a lightweight pre-processing step.

---

## Top Show HN Repos — Weighted Ranking

**Source:** HN Algolia API, Jan 1 – Apr 8, 2026, Show HN posts linking to GitHub repos.
**Formula:** `score = upvotes × 3 + stars × 0.1` (upvotes weighted ~30x per unit)

|------|-------|------|---------|-------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | 3031 | [KittenML/KittenTTS](https://github.com/KittenML/KittenTTS) | 561 | 13.5K | Python | TTS: text → normalize → phoneme → acoustic model → vocoder → audio |
| 2 | 1916 | [manaflow-ai/cmux](https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux) | 198 | 13.2K | Swift? | Terminal: input → tab mgmt → PTY → render → notifications |
| 3 | 1841 | [puemos/craftplan](https://github.com/puemos/craftplan) | 577 | 1.1K | Web | Production mgmt: request → auth → scheduling → inventory → response |
| 4 | 1631 | [rowboatlabs/rowboat](https://github.com/rowboatlabs/rowboat) | 205 | 10.2K | Python/TS | AI agent: input → entity extraction → graph → context → LLM → response |
| 5 | 1277 | [htdt/godogen](https://github.com/htdt/godogen) | 337 | 2.7K | GDScript | Game gen: prompt → scene → nodes → scripts → project assembly |
| 6 | 1103 | [localgpt-app/localgpt](https://github.com/localgpt-app/localgpt) | 331 | 1.1K | Rust | Local AI: request → memory → context → inference → response |
| 7 | 1006 | [valdanylchuk/breezydemo](https://github.com/valdanylchuk/breezydemo) | 327 | 247 | C/Embedded | ESP32: boot → shell → input → command parse → execute → display |
| 8 | 993 | [generalaction/emdash](https://github.com/generalaction/emdash) | 206 | 3.7K | TS | Dev env: file watch → parse → agent loop → edit → validate |
| 9 | 971 | [timescale/pg_textsearch](https://github.com/timescale/pg_textsearch) | 203 | 3.6K | Rust/C | Search: query → tokenize → BM25 score → index scan → rank → results |
| 10 | 891 | [19-84/redd-archiver](https://github.com/19-84/redd-archiver) | 286 | 330 | ? | Archive: fetch → parse → store → index → serve |
| 11 | 881 | [moongate-community/moongatev2](https://github.com/moongate-community/moongatev2) | 287 | 199 | C#/.NET | Game server: connect → packet → state → simulate → response |
| 12 | 864 | [OlaProeis/Ferrite](https://github.com/OlaProeis/Ferrite) | 241 | 1.4K | Rust | Editor: input → parse → AST → render → Mermaid diagrams |
| 13 | 836 | [IronsideXXVI/Hacker-News](https://github.com/IronsideXXVI/Hacker-News) | 265 | 409 | Swift | Mobile: API → JSON parse → SwiftUI state → view render |
| 14 | 710 | [odvcencio/gotreesitter](https://github.com/odvcencio/gotreesitter) | 222 | 443 | Go | Parser: source → lexer → parse → tree → query |
| 15 | 653 | [onecli/onecli](https://github.com/onecli/onecli) | 161 | 1.7K | Rust | Vault: request → auth → secret mgmt → agent context |
| 16 | 635 | [yannick-cw/korb](https://github.com/yannick-cw/korb) | 204 | 225 | Haskell | CLI: auth → reverse-eng API → cart → checkout → order |
| 17 | 456 | [beginner-jhj/mini_browser](https://github.com/beginner-jhj/mini_browser) | 146 | 177 | C++ | Browser: HTML → DOM → CSS → layout → paint → render |

### Recommended 10 (optimizing for pipeline clarity + domain diversity)

Excluded domains already covered: gateway/API (OpenClaw), search (qmd), agent loop (pi-mono), database (SpacetimeDB).

| # | Repo | Domain | Why it's a good trail |
|---|------|--------|----------------------|
| 1 | **KittenTTS** | ML/TTS | Clear ML pipeline, massive traction, unique domain |
| 2 | **Craftplan** | Production mgmt | Highest upvotes, web app with scheduling pipeline |
| 3 | **LocalGPT** | Local AI (Rust) | Rust inference pipeline, persistent memory adds depth |
| 4 | **pg_textsearch** | Postgres extension | BM25 scoring pipeline, Rust extension for Postgres — very teachable |
| 5 | **Moongate** | Game server | .NET game server pipeline, packet parsing → world sim — unique domain |
| 6 | **Ferrite** | Text editor (Rust) | Markdown → AST → render pipeline, Mermaid rendering |
| 7 | **gotreesitter** | Compiler/parser (Go) | Classic lexer → parser → tree pipeline, educational |
| 8 | **mini_browser** | Browser engine (C++) | HTML → DOM → CSS → layout → paint — the canonical pipeline |
| 9 | **korb** | CLI (Haskell) | Reverse-engineered API, Haskell is unique, fun domain |
| 10 | **cmux** | Terminal (Swift) | Huge stars, terminal multiplexer pipeline |

- **breezydemo** (score 1006) — embedded/ESP32 may be hard to trace as a web-style pipeline
- **redd-archiver** (score 891) — simpler fetch/store pipeline, less teachable depth

---

## Implementation Approach

For each of the 10 repos:

1. **Pre-analyze with DeepWiki** (optional accelerator)
   - Call `read_wiki_structure` via MCP to get architectural TOC
   - Identify pipeline-relevant sections
   
2. **Run trail-data-extractor agent** against the repo
   - Pass DeepWiki context if available
   - Agent detects framework, maps pipeline to ~8 stops, extracts code + narration
   
3. **Generate game HTML**
   - Create `{game}/index.html` with embedded TRAIL_DATA
   - Add custom TRAIL_FLAVORS and TRAIL_CONFIG
   
4. **Update index**
   - Add to TRAILS array in `index.html`
   - Update memory file with repo mapping

---

## Verification

- Run `scripts/audit-trails.js` to validate all new trail data
- Open each game in browser to verify playability
- Check that index.html lists all 23 trails (13 existing + 10 new)

---

## Open Questions

1. Should we integrate DeepWiki MCP into the trail-data-extractor agent prompt, or keep it as a manual pre-step?
2. Any repos in the recommended 10 you'd swap out? (e.g., swap korb for Rowboat?)
3. Should we batch all 10, or start with the top 3-5 and iterate?

input.new_string:
## Implementation Plan

### Step 1: Integrate DeepWiki into trail-data-extractor

Modify `agents/trail-data-extractor.md` to add a DeepWiki pre-analysis phase:
- Add DeepWiki MCP server config to agent's tool list
- Before analyzing a repo's code, call `read_wiki_structure` to get the architectural TOC
- Use the TOC to identify pipeline-relevant sections and guide which files to prioritize
- This gives the agent a structural map before diving into code

**Key files:**
- `agents/trail-data-extractor.md` — add DeepWiki instructions + MCP config

### Step 2: Generate trail games for all 10 repos

For each repo, in this order:

| # | Repo | Game dir | Trail name (tentative) |
|---|------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | KittenML/KittenTTS | `kittentts/` | The Synthesis Trail |
| 2 | puemos/craftplan | `craftplan/` | The Production Trail |
| 3 | localgpt-app/localgpt | `localgpt/` | The Inference Trail |
| 4 | timescale/pg_textsearch | `pg-textsearch/` | The Relevance Trail |
| 5 | moongate-community/moongatev2 | `moongate/` | The Packet Trail |
| 6 | OlaProeis/Ferrite | `ferrite/` | The Rendering Trail |
| 7 | odvcencio/gotreesitter | `gotreesitter/` | The Parser Trail |
| 8 | beginner-jhj/mini_browser | `mini-browser/` | The Layout Trail |
| 9 | yannick-cw/korb | `korb/` | The Checkout Trail |
| 10 | manaflow-ai/cmux | `cmux/` | The Terminal Trail |

For each:
1. Clone/fetch the repo (or use GitHub API to read files)
2. Run trail-data-extractor agent (now with DeepWiki pre-analysis)
3. Generate `{game}/index.html` with TRAIL_DATA, TRAIL_FLAVORS, TRAIL_CONFIG
4. Add to TRAILS array in root `index.html`

### Step 3: Update index and metadata

- Add all 10 new entries to the TRAILS array in `index.html` (with colors, subtitles)
- Update memory file with repo mappings

---

## Verification

- Run `node scripts/audit-trails.js` to validate all new trail data
- Open each game in browser to verify playability
- Check that `index.html` lists all 23 trails (13 existing + 10 new)
- Verify DeepWiki integration works by checking agent output includes architectural context

---
description: Detects the web framework in a repository and extracts trail data for the Coderegon Trail game
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash(git:*), Bash(gh:*)
model: sonnet
---

# Trail Data Extractor Agent

You analyze a codebase to detect its web framework and extract structured trail data for the Coderegon Trail game. The trail maps the framework's request/render pipeline to ~8 stops, with code snippets, narration, and quiz events at each stop.

## Your Task

Given a target (framework name or repository path), produce a JSON trail data object that the Coderegon Trail game can consume.

## Modes

### Canonical Mode (framework name provided)
When the user specifies a framework by name (e.g., "next", "rails", "django"), return the pre-built canonical trail from `skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md`.

Read the framework-trails reference file and format its data as the JSON structure described in the Output Format section below.

### Repo Mode (repository path provided — Phase 3)
When analyzing a real repository:

1. **Detect framework**: Look for telltale files and dependencies
   - `next.config.js` / `next.config.mjs` → Next.js
   - `Gemfile` with `rails` gem → Rails
   - `manage.py` + `settings.py` → Django
   - `package.json` with `express` → Express
   - `package.json` with `react` (no Next.js) + `vite.config` → React/Vite
   - `composer.json` with `laravel/framework` → Laravel

2. **Map pipeline stages**: For the detected framework, identify the real files that correspond to each trail stop:
   - Find the entry point (main file, index, config.ru, etc.)
   - Trace the middleware/interceptor chain
   - Find routing configuration
   - Identify controller/handler files
   - Find model/data layer
   - Find view/template/component files
   - Find the response formation

3. **Extract code snippets**: For each stop, extract the most representative 15-25 lines from the mapped file. Prefer:
   - Complete function definitions
   - Configuration that shows the framework's conventions
   - Code that demonstrates the pipeline stage's purpose

4. **Generate narration**: For each snippet, write 3-5 sentences of TTS-friendly narration following the `narration` skill guidelines:
   - Conversational tone
   - Explain WHY, not just WHAT
   - Sentences under 25 words
   - Use "slash" for path separators
   - Never read code syntax literally

5. **Generate events**: Create 4-6 quiz events based on the actual code:
   - Mix event types: weather, river, encounter, misfortune
   - Questions should test understanding of the real code patterns used
   - Include 3 choices per event with explanations
   - Map each event to one of the 4 party member concepts

## Framework Detection Confidence

Report detection confidence:
- **high**: Clear framework-specific files found (e.g., `next.config.js`)
- **medium**: Dependencies suggest framework but structure is non-standard
- **low**: Ambiguous — multiple frameworks possible or custom setup

If confidence is low, ask the user to specify the framework.

## Output Format

Return a single `TRAIL_DATA:` block containing valid JSON:

```
TRAIL_DATA:
{
  "framework": "nextjs",
  "trailName": "The App Router Trail",
  "confidence": "high",
  "stops": [
    {
      "name": "Independence, MO",
      "subtitle": "The Entry Point",
      "concept": "Project initialization",
      "landmarkType": "town",
      "code": {
        "file": "app/layout.tsx",
        "startLine": 1,
        "endLine": 18,
        "content": "import type { Metadata } from 'next'\n...",
        "language": "typescript"
      },
      "narration": "Every Next.js journey starts here..."
    }
  ],
  "events": [
    {
      "type": "weather",
      "trigger": "after_stop",
      "triggerStop": 1,
      "title": "Middleware Storm!",
      "text": "A storm of requests...",
      "choices": [
        {
          "text": "Add a matcher config...",
          "correct": true,
          "explanation": "The config.matcher property..."
        },
        {
          "text": "Move middleware to pages...",
          "correct": false,
          "explanation": "That would mean duplicating..."
        },
        {
          "text": "Cache middleware results...",
          "correct": false,
          "explanation": "Middleware runs per-request..."
        }
      ],
      "concept": "File Router",
      "difficulty": "easy"
    }
  ],
  "partyMembers": [
    { "name": "Server Components", "icon": "server", "maxHealth": 3 },
    { "name": "File Router", "icon": "router", "maxHealth": 3 },
    { "name": "Layouts", "icon": "layout", "maxHealth": 3 },
    { "name": "Data Fetching", "icon": "data", "maxHealth": 3 }
  ],
  "deathMessages": [
    "Here lies your app. It died of unhandled promise rejections.",
    "Drowned in the hydration mismatch river."
  ]
}
```

## Party Member Mapping

Each framework has 4 party members representing key concepts:

| Framework | Members |
|-----------|---------|
| Next.js | Server Components, File Router, Layouts, Data Fetching |
| Rails | ActiveRecord, Convention Routing, ERB Templates, Migrations |
| Django | ORM QuerySets, URL Patterns, Template Inheritance, Admin Panel |
| Express | Middleware Chain, Route Handlers, Error Handling, Async Patterns |
| React/Vite | Component Tree, Hooks, State Management, Effect Lifecycle |
| Laravel | Eloquent ORM, Blade Templates, Service Container, Artisan CLI |
| OpenClaw | Gateway Control Plane, Channel Plugins, Route Resolution, Session Persistence |

## Narration Guidelines

Follow the `narration` skill for all narration text:
- Conversational: "Let's look at...", "Notice how...", "The reason for this is..."
- Educational: explain WHY, not just WHAT
- TTS-friendly: sentences under 25 words, "slash" for paths, spell out abbreviations
- Progressive: overview first, details available on demand
- No code syntax: never say "const x equals..."

# Fly Visual — Coderegon Trail Game

You generate and launch a retro pixel art Oregon Trail-style game that teaches a web framework's request pipeline — or reviews a pull request. The game is a self-contained HTML file opened in the browser.

**User input:** "$ARGUMENTS"

Use the `visualization` skill for game generation guidelines. Follow the trail data in `skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md` for framework trails, and `skills/visualization/references/pr-trail.md` for PR trails.

## Step 1: Parse Arguments and Determine Mode

Parse the user's input. **Check for PR inputs first** (before framework name matching):

content.content:
30	Parse the user's input. **Check for PR inputs first** (before framework name matching):
31	
32	| Input | Detection | Mode | Action |
33	|-------|-----------|------|--------|
34	| `123` (purely numeric) | PR number | PR | → pr-trail-extractor |
35	| URL containing `/pull/` | PR URL | PR | → pr-trail-extractor (extract PR number from URL) |
36	| Contains `..` (e.g., `main..feature`) | Git range | PR | → pr-trail-extractor |
37	| `HEAD~N` pattern | Recent commits | PR | → pr-trail-extractor |
38	| `next`, `nextjs`, `next.js` | Framework name | Framework | The App Router Trail |
39	| `rails`, `ruby`, `ror` | Framework name | Framework | The Convention Trail |
40	| `django`, `python` | Framework name | Framework | The WSGI Wagon Trail |
41	| `express`, `node` | Framework name | Framework | The Middleware Prairie |
42	| `react`, `vite` | Framework name | Framework | The Component Canyon |
43	| `laravel`, `php` | Framework name | Framework | The Artisan Trail |
44	| `openclaw`, `claw`, `gateway` | Framework name | Framework | The Gateway Trail |
45	| (empty) | No input | Framework | Auto-detect from repo |
46	
47	### Auto-Detection (if no framework specified)
48	Spawn a `trail-data-extractor` agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose) to detect the framework from the current repository. If detection confidence is low, ask the user to specify.
49	
50	## Step 2: Extract Trail Data
51	
52	### For PR Inputs (PR number, URL, git range, HEAD~N)
53	1. Spawn a `pr-trail-extractor` agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose)
54	2. Pass the PR source (number, URL, or range) to the agent
55	3. Agent fetches the diff, groups changes, and returns a TRAIL_DATA JSON block with `trailTheme: "pr"`
56	4. Parse the returned JSON
57	
58	### For Canonical Trails (framework specified by name)
59	1. Read `skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md`
60	2. Extract the trail data for the specified framework
61	3. Format it as the TRAIL_DATA JSON structure defined in the visualization skill
62	
63	### For Repo Analysis (auto-detected or Phase 3)
64	1. Spawn a `trail-data-extractor` agent (via Task tool, subagent_type: general-purpose)
65	2. Agent analyzes the repo and returns a TRAIL_DATA JSON block
66	3. Parse the returned JSON
67	
68	## Step 3: Generate the HTML Game
69	
70	Use the `frontend-design` skill (via Skill tool) to generate the complete HTML game file.
71	
72	Provide the frontend-design skill with these instructions:
73	
74	---
75	
76	**Generate a self-contained HTML file for the Coderegon Trail game.**
77	
78	### Game Requirements
79	
80	1. **Single HTML file** — all CSS, JavaScript, and game data inline. No external dependencies.
81	2. **Canvas + DOM hybrid** — use Canvas for the scrolling landscape and wagon animation, DOM for UI panels (code display, narration, choices, status bar).
82	3. **Game state machine** — implement states: TITLE, SETUP, TRAVEL, STOP, EVENT, RIVER, DEATH, WIN
83	4. **Pixel art aesthetic** — follow the style guide in the visualization skill references
84	
85	### Trail Data
86	
87	Embed this trail data as a JavaScript constant:
88	
89	```javascript
90	const TRAIL_DATA = {TRAIL_DATA_JSON};
91	```
92	
93	### Screen Layout
94	
95	```
96	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
97	│  Scrolling landscape with parallax          │  40% height — Canvas
98	│  Mountains (0.3x) → Trees (0.6x) → Ground  │
99	│  Wagon centered on trail                    │
100	├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
101	│  Code panel OR narration OR event choices   │  45% height — DOM
102	├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
103	│  Health bar | Supplies | Morale | Party     │  15% height — DOM
104	│  Stop N of M — Current Stop Name            │
105	└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
106	```
107	
108	### Game Flow Implementation
109	
110	**Title Screen:**
111	- "CODEREGON TRAIL" in pixel-style text (bold monospace, letter-spacing)
112	- Framework trail name as subtitle
113	- Scrolling landscape animation behind title
114	- "Click to begin" prompt
115	
116	**Setup Phase:**
117	- Show 4 party members with their concept names
118	- Each has a pixel portrait (simple colored square icons)
119	- Player can rename (optional) or keep default names
120	- Difficulty selection: Settler / Adventurer / Trail Blazer
121	- "Hit the trail!" button
122	
123	**Travel Animation:**
124	- Wagon scrolls across landscape (2-3 seconds)
125	- Parallax scrolling: 3 layers at different speeds
126	- Day counter and progress indicator update
127	- Random travel flavor text
128	
129	**Stop Phase:**

## Step 2: Extract Trail Data

### For PR Inputs (PR number, URL, git range, HEAD~N)

---

**Generate a self-contained HTML file for the Coderegon Trail game.**

### Game Requirements

1. **Single HTML file** — all CSS, JavaScript, and game data inline. No external dependencies.
2. **Canvas + DOM hybrid** — use Canvas for the scrolling landscape and wagon animation, DOM for UI panels (code display, narration, choices, status bar).

### Trail Data

**Travel Animation:**

- Parallax scrolling: 3 layers at different speeds
- Day counter and progress indicator update

---
description: Detects the web framework in a repository and extracts trail data for the Coderegon Trail game
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash(git:*), Bash(gh:*)
model: sonnet
---

# Trail Data Extractor Agent

You analyze a codebase to detect its web framework and extract structured trail data for the Coderegon Trail game. The trail maps the framework's request/render pipeline to ~8 stops, with code snippets, narration, and quiz events at each stop.

## Your Task

Given a target (framework name or repository path), produce a JSON trail data object that the Coderegon Trail game can consume.

## Modes

### Canonical Mode (framework name provided)
When the user specifies a framework by name (e.g., "next", "rails", "django"), return the pre-built canonical trail from `skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md`.

Read the framework-trails reference file and format its data as the JSON structure described in the Output Format section below.

### Repo Mode (repository path provided — Phase 3)
When analyzing a real repository:

1. **Detect framework**: Look for telltale files and dependencies
   - `next.config.js` / `next.config.mjs` → Next.js
   - `Gemfile` with `rails` gem → Rails
   - `manage.py` + `settings.py` → Django
   - `package.json` with `express` → Express
   - `package.json` with `react` (no Next.js) + `vite.config` → React/Vite
   - `composer.json` with `laravel/framework` → Laravel

2. **Map pipeline stages**: For the detected framework, identify the real files that correspond to each trail stop:
   - Find the entry point (main file, index, config.ru, etc.)
   - Trace the middleware/interceptor chain
   - Find routing configuration
   - Identify controller/handler files
   - Find model/data layer
   - Find view/template/component files
   - Find the response formation

3. **Extract code snippets**: For each stop, extract the most representative 15-25 lines from the mapped file. Prefer:
   - Complete function definitions
   - Configuration that shows the framework's conventions
   - Code that demonstrates the pipeline stage's purpose

4. **Generate narration**: For each snippet, write 3-5 sentences of TTS-friendly narration following the `narration` skill guidelines:
   - Conversational tone
   - Explain WHY, not just WHAT
   - Sentences under 25 words
   - Use "slash" for path separators
   - Never read code syntax literally

5. **Generate events**: Create 4-6 quiz events based on the actual code:
   - Mix event types: weather, river, encounter, misfortune
   - Questions should test understanding of the real code patterns used
   - Include 3 choices per event with explanations
   - Map each event to one of the 4 party member concepts

## Framework Detection Confidence

Report detection confidence:
- **high**: Clear framework-specific files found (e.g., `next.config.js`)
- **medium**: Dependencies suggest framework but structure is non-standard
- **low**: Ambiguous — multiple frameworks possible or custom setup

If confidence is low, ask the user to specify the framework.

## Output Format

Return a single `TRAIL_DATA:` block containing valid JSON:

```
TRAIL_DATA:
{
  "framework": "nextjs",
  "trailName": "The App Router Trail",
  "confidence": "high",
  "stops": [
    {
      "name": "Independence, MO",
      "subtitle": "The Entry Point",
      "concept": "Project initialization",
      "landmarkType": "town",
      "code": {
        "file": "app/layout.tsx",
        "startLine": 1,
        "endLine": 18,
        "content": "import type { Metadata } from 'next'\n...",
        "language": "typescript"
      },
      "narration": "Every Next.js journey starts here..."
    }
  ],
  "events": [
    {
      "type": "weather",
      "trigger": "after_stop",
      "triggerStop": 1,
      "title": "Middleware Storm!",
      "text": "A storm of requests...",
      "choices": [
        {
          "text": "Add a matcher config...",
          "correct": true,
          "explanation": "The config.matcher property..."
        },
        {
          "text": "Move middleware to pages...",
          "correct": false,
          "explanation": "That would mean duplicating..."
        },
        {
          "text": "Cache middleware results...",
          "correct": false,
          "explanation": "Middleware runs per-request..."
        }
      ],
      "concept": "File Router",
      "difficulty": "easy"
    }
  ],
  "partyMembers": [
    { "name": "Server Components", "icon": "server", "maxHealth": 3 },
    { "name": "File Router", "icon": "router", "maxHealth": 3 },
    { "name": "Layouts", "icon": "layout", "maxHealth": 3 },
    { "name": "Data Fetching", "icon": "data", "maxHealth": 3 }
  ],
  "deathMessages": [
    "Here lies your app. It died of unhandled promise rejections.",
    "Drowned in the hydration mismatch river."
  ]
}
```

## Party Member Mapping

Each framework has 4 party members representing key concepts:

| Framework | Members |
|-----------|---------|
| Next.js | Server Components, File Router, Layouts, Data Fetching |
| Rails | ActiveRecord, Convention Routing, ERB Templates, Migrations |
| Django | ORM QuerySets, URL Patterns, Template Inheritance, Admin Panel |
| Express | Middleware Chain, Route Handlers, Error Handling, Async Patterns |
| React/Vite | Component Tree, Hooks, State Management, Effect Lifecycle |
| Laravel | Eloquent ORM, Blade Templates, Service Container, Artisan CLI |
| OpenClaw | Gateway Control Plane, Channel Plugins, Route Resolution, Session Persistence |

## Narration Guidelines

Follow the `narration` skill for all narration text:
- Conversational: "Let's look at...", "Notice how...", "The reason for this is..."
- Educational: explain WHY, not just WHAT
- TTS-friendly: sentences under 25 words, "slash" for paths, spell out abbreviations
- Progressive: overview first, details available on demand
- No code syntax: never say "const x equals..."

### Repo Mode (repository path provided — Phase 3)
When analyzing a real repository:

0. **DeepWiki pre-analysis** (optional accelerator): If the repo is on GitHub, fetch its architectural wiki structure from DeepWiki to get a head start on understanding the pipeline. This step is best-effort — skip if it fails or returns nothing useful.

   ```bash
   # Fetch wiki table of contents for owner/repo
   curl -s -X POST https://mcp.deepwiki.com/mcp \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -H "Accept: application/json" \
     -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"read_wiki_structure","arguments":{"repoName":"OWNER/REPO"}},"id":1}' \
     | jq -r '.result.content[0].text // empty'
   ```

   The TOC reveals the repo's architectural sections — look for pipeline-related entries like "Request Lifecycle", "Middleware Flow", "Processing Pipeline", "Architecture", etc. Use these to guide which files to prioritize in steps 1-3 below.

   If the TOC identifies clear pipeline stages, you can optionally fetch the full wiki content for deeper context (warning: can be very large):
   ```bash
   curl -s -X POST https://mcp.deepwiki.com/mcp \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -H "Accept: application/json" \
     -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"read_wiki_contents","arguments":{"repoName":"OWNER/REPO"}},"id":1}' \
     | jq -r '.result.content[0].text // empty' | head -500
   ```

   Use any pipeline/architecture insights from DeepWiki to inform the steps below — it helps you identify the right files faster and write better narration.

1. **Detect framework**: Look for telltale files and dependencies
   - `next.config.js` / `next.config.mjs` → Next.js
   - `Gemfile` with `rails` gem → Rails
   - `manage.py` + `settings.py` → Django
   - `package.json` with `express` → Express
   - `package.json` with `react` (no Next.js) + `vite.config` → React/Vite
   - `composer.json` with `laravel/framework` → Laravel

| Framework | Members |
|-----------|---------|
| Next.js | Server Components, File Router, Layouts, Data Fetching |
| Rails | ActiveRecord, Convention Routing, ERB Templates, Migrations |
| Django | ORM QuerySets, URL Patterns, Template Inheritance, Admin Panel |
| Express | Middleware Chain, Route Handlers, Error Handling, Async Patterns |
| React/Vite | Component Tree, Hooks, State Management, Effect Lifecycle |
| Laravel | Eloquent ORM, Blade Templates, Service Container, Artisan CLI |
| OpenClaw | Gateway Control Plane, Channel Plugins, Route Resolution, Session Persistence |

## Narration Guidelines

Follow the `narration` skill for all narration text:
- Conversational: "Let's look at...", "Notice how...", "The reason for this is..."
- Educational: explain WHY, not just WHAT
- TTS-friendly: sentences under 25 words, "slash" for paths, spell out abbreviations
- Progressive: overview first, details available on demand
- No code syntax: never say "const x equals..."

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Generate a game HTML file from a trail data JSON file + customizations
# Usage: ./scripts/generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>
#
# trail-data.json: The TRAIL_DATA JSON object
# customizations.js: File containing:
#   - TITLE: single line with the <title> text
#   - FLAVORS: the travelFlavors array (JS)
#   - OVERLAYS: the drawEventOverlay function body (JS)

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
TEMPLATE="$PROJECT_ROOT/openclaw/index.html"

GAME_DIR="${1:?Usage: generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>}"
TRAIL_DATA_FILE="${2:?Missing trail-data.json}"
CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE="${3:?Missing customizations.js}"

# Create game directory
mkdir -p "$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR"

OUTPUT="$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR/index.html"

# Read customizations file sections
TITLE=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ TITLE$/,/^\/\/ END TITLE$/{ /^\/\//d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
FLAVORS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ FLAVORS$/,/^\/\/ END FLAVORS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?FLAVORS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
OVERLAYS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ OVERLAYS$/,/^\/\/ END OVERLAYS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?OVERLAYS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Generate a game HTML file from a trail data JSON file + customizations
# Usage: ./scripts/generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>
#
# trail-data.json: The TRAIL_DATA JSON object
# customizations.js: File containing:
#   - TITLE: single line with the <title> text
#   - FLAVORS: the travelFlavors array (JS)
#   - OVERLAYS: the drawEventOverlay function body (JS)

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
TEMPLATE="$PROJECT_ROOT/openclaw/index.html"

GAME_DIR="${1:?Usage: generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>}"
TRAIL_DATA_FILE="${2:?Missing trail-data.json}"
CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE="${3:?Missing customizations.js}"

# Create game directory
mkdir -p "$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR"

OUTPUT="$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR/index.html"

# Read customizations file sections
TITLE=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ TITLE$/,/^\/\/ END TITLE$/{ /^\/\//d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
FLAVORS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ FLAVORS$/,/^\/\/ END FLAVORS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?FLAVORS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
OVERLAYS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ OVERLAYS$/,/^\/\/ END OVERLAYS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?OVERLAYS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")

# Read trail data
TRAIL_DATA=$(cat "$TRAIL_DATA_FILE")

# Build the game HTML by extracting template sections and inserting custom parts
{
  # Lines 1-5: HTML header before title
  sed -n '1,5p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Custom title
  echo "<title>$TITLE</title>"

  # Lines 7-137: CSS and HTML structure after title
  sed -n '7,137p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Trail data line
  echo "const TRAIL_DATA=$TRAIL_DATA;"

  # Lines 139-188: Game state (identical across games)
  sed -n '139,188p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Custom travel flavors
  echo "$FLAVORS"

  # Lines 206-489: Shared engine (canvas, rendering, etc.) up to overlay function header
  sed -n '206,489p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Custom event overlays (inside drawEventOverlay function)
  echo "$OVERLAYS"

  # Close drawEventOverlay function + rest of engine
  echo "}"
  echo ""
  sed -n '711,$p' "$TEMPLATE"

} > "$OUTPUT"

echo "Generated $OUTPUT ($(wc -c < "$OUTPUT" | tr -d ' ') bytes)"

```bash
curl -s -X POST https://mcp.deepwiki.com/mcp \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json" \

```bash
gh api repos/localgpt-app/localgpt --jq '.description, .language, .default_branch'

# Validate JSON for completed trail-data files
for d in kittentts craftplan pg-textsearch ferrite mini-browser korb cmux; do
  f="/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/.claude/worktrees/hn-popular/$d/trail-data.json"
  if jq -e '.stops | length' "$f" 2>/dev/null; then
    stops=$(jq '.stops | length' "$f")
    events=$(jq '.events | length' "$f")
    party=$(jq '.partyMembers | length' "$f")
    echo "$d: OK - $stops stops, $events events, $party party members"
  else
    echo "$d: INVALID JSON"
  fi
done

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	set -euo pipefail
3	
4	# Generate a game HTML file from a trail data JSON file + customizations
5	# Usage: ./scripts/generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>
6	#
7	# trail-data.json: The TRAIL_DATA JSON object
8	# customizations.js: File containing:
9	#   - TITLE: single line with the <title> text
10	#   - FLAVORS: the travelFlavors array (JS)
11	#   - OVERLAYS: the drawEventOverlay function body (JS)
12	
13	SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
14	PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
15	TEMPLATE="$PROJECT_ROOT/openclaw/index.html"
16	
17	GAME_DIR="${1:?Usage: generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>}"
18	TRAIL_DATA_FILE="${2:?Missing trail-data.json}"
19	CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE="${3:?Missing customizations.js}"
20	
21	# Create game directory
22	mkdir -p "$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR"
23	
24	OUTPUT="$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR/index.html"
25	
26	# Read customizations file sections
27	TITLE=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ TITLE$/,/^\/\/ END TITLE$/{ /^\/\//d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
28	FLAVORS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ FLAVORS$/,/^\/\/ END FLAVORS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?FLAVORS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
29	OVERLAYS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ OVERLAYS$/,/^\/\/ END OVERLAYS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?OVERLAYS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
30	
31	# Read trail data
32	TRAIL_DATA=$(cat "$TRAIL_DATA_FILE")
33	
34	# Build the game HTML by extracting template sections and inserting custom parts
35	{
36	  # Lines 1-5: HTML header before title
37	  sed -n '1,5p' "$TEMPLATE"
38	
39	  # Custom title
40	  echo "<title>$TITLE</title>"
41	
42	  # Lines 7-137: CSS and HTML structure after title
43	  sed -n '7,137p' "$TEMPLATE"
44	
45	  # Trail data line
46	  echo "const TRAIL_DATA=$TRAIL_DATA;"
47	
48	  # Lines 139-188: Game state (identical across games)
49	  sed -n '139,188p' "$TEMPLATE"
50	
51	  # Custom travel flavors
52	  echo "$FLAVORS"
53	
54	  # Lines 206-489: Shared engine (canvas, rendering, etc.) up to overlay function header
55	  sed -n '206,489p' "$TEMPLATE"
56	
57	  # Custom event overlays (inside drawEventOverlay function)
58	  echo "$OVERLAYS"
59	
60	  # Close drawEventOverlay function + rest of engine
61	  echo "}"
62	  echo ""
63	  sed -n '711,$p' "$TEMPLATE"
64	
65	} > "$OUTPUT"
66	
67	echo "Generated $OUTPUT ($(wc -c < "$OUTPUT" | tr -d ' ') bytes)"
68

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Generate a game HTML file from a trail data JSON file + customizations
# Usage: ./scripts/generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>
#
# trail-data.json: The TRAIL_DATA JSON object
# customizations.js: File containing:
#   - TITLE: single line with the <title> text
#   - FLAVORS: the travelFlavors array (JS)
#   - OVERLAYS: the drawEventOverlay function body (JS)

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
TEMPLATE="$PROJECT_ROOT/openclaw/index.html"

GAME_DIR="${1:?Usage: generate-game.sh <game-dir> <trail-data.json> <customizations.js>}"
TRAIL_DATA_FILE="${2:?Missing trail-data.json}"
CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE="${3:?Missing customizations.js}"

# Create game directory
mkdir -p "$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR"

OUTPUT="$PROJECT_ROOT/$GAME_DIR/index.html"

# Read customizations file sections
TITLE=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ TITLE$/,/^\/\/ END TITLE$/{ /^\/\//d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
FLAVORS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ FLAVORS$/,/^\/\/ END FLAVORS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?FLAVORS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")
OVERLAYS=$(sed -n '/^\/\/ OVERLAYS$/,/^\/\/ END OVERLAYS$/{ /^\/\/ \(END \)\?OVERLAYS$/d; p; }' "$CUSTOMIZATIONS_FILE")

# Read trail data
TRAIL_DATA=$(cat "$TRAIL_DATA_FILE")

# Build the game HTML by extracting template sections and inserting custom parts
{
  # Lines 1-5: HTML header before title
  sed -n '1,5p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Custom title
  echo "<title>$TITLE</title>"

  # Lines 7-137: CSS and HTML structure after title
  sed -n '7,137p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Trail data line
  echo "const TRAIL_DATA=$TRAIL_DATA;"

  # Lines 139-188: Game state (identical across games)
  sed -n '139,188p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Custom travel flavors
  echo "$FLAVORS"

  # Lines 206-489: Shared engine (canvas, rendering, etc.) up to overlay function header
  sed -n '206,489p' "$TEMPLATE"

  # Custom event overlays (inside drawEventOverlay function)
  echo "$OVERLAYS"

  # Close drawEventOverlay function + rest of engine
  echo "}"
  echo ""
  sed -n '711,$p' "$TEMPLATE"

} > "$OUTPUT"

echo "Generated $OUTPUT ($(wc -c < "$OUTPUT" | tr -d ' ') bytes)"

input.content:
---
name: DeepWiki Integration
description: DeepWiki MCP server provides free architectural analysis of any public GitHub repo, useful for pre-analyzing repos before trail data extraction
type: reference
---

DeepWiki (by Cognition/Devin) generates architectural wikis for public GitHub repos.

**MCP endpoint:** `https://mcp.deepwiki.com/mcp` (Streamable HTTP, no auth needed)

**Useful tools:**
- `read_wiki_structure` — returns hierarchical TOC, helps identify pipeline stages
- `read_wiki_contents` — full wiki as markdown with diagrams + file:line refs (can be 1MB+)
- `ask_question` — AI Q&A grounded in wiki (unreliable as of 2026-04)

**How to call via curl:**
```bash
curl -s -X POST https://mcp.deepwiki.com/mcp \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"read_wiki_structure","arguments":{"repoName":"OWNER/REPO"}},"id":1}'
```

**Why:** Accelerates trail-data-extractor by providing pre-analyzed architecture before code reading.
**How to apply:** Integrated into `agents/trail-data-extractor.md` as step 0 of Repo Mode. Best-effort — skip if it fails.

input.command:
# Comprehensive validation of all 10 new games
echo "=== Game File Validation ==="
echo ""
for d in kittentts craftplan localgpt pg-textsearch moongate ferrite gotreesitter mini-browser korb cmux; do
  f="/Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/.claude/worktrees/hn-popular/$d/index.html"
  if [ ! -f "$f" ]; then
    echo "$d: MISSING index.html"
    continue
  fi
  size=$(wc -c < "$f" | tr -d ' ')
  lines=$(wc -l < "$f" | tr -d ' ')
  has_title=$(grep -c '<title>' "$f")
  has_trail=$(grep -c 'TRAIL_DATA' "$f")
  has_flavors=$(grep -c 'travelFlavors' "$f")
  has_overlay=$(grep -c 'drawEventOverlay' "$f")
  has_states=$(grep -c 'STATES.TITLE' "$f")
  echo "$d: OK - ${size}B, ${lines} lines | title:$has_title trail:$has_trail flavors:$has_flavors overlay:$has_overlay states:$has_states"
done

echo ""
echo "=== Index Validation ==="
trails_count=$(grep -c 'href:.*/' /Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/.claude/worktrees/hn-popular/index.html)
fallback_count=$(grep -c 'class="tc"' /Users/flowclub/code/coderegon-trail/.claude/worktrees/hn-popular/index.html)
echo "TRAILS array entries: $trails_count"
echo "Fallback links: $fallback_count"

content.text:
All 10 research agents completed successfully. The games were already assembled earlier — everything is done. Here's the final state:

**10 new trail games** with real code from each repo:

| Trail | Repo | Lang | Stops | Events | Real Code |
|-------|------|------|-------|--------|-----------|
| The Synthesis Trail | KittenML/KittenTTS | Python | 8 | 7 | TTS pipeline: model loading → preprocessing → phonemizer → ONNX inference → audio |
| The Production Trail | puemos/craftplan | Elixir | 8 | 7 | Phoenix/Ash: endpoint → router → LiveView auth → product catalog → orders → forecasting |
| The Inference Trail | localgpt-app/localgpt | Rust | 8 | 7 | AI agent: HTTP gateway → memory → embedding search → prompt assembly → providers → tools |
| The Relevance Trail | timescale/pg_textsearch | C | 8 | 8 | BM25: query parsing → planner hooks → tokenization → IDF → memtable → Block-Max WAND |
| The Packet Trail | moongate-community/moongatev2 | C# | 8 | 7 | UO server: bootstrap → TCP → Twofish encryption → middleware → packet dispatch → game loop |
| The Rendering Trail | OlaProeis/Ferrite | Rust | 8 | 7 | Editor: rope buffer → comrak AST → blake3 cache → syntect → Mermaid → virtual scroll |
| The Parser Trail | odvcencio/gotreesitter | Go | 8 | 7 | Parser: grammar registry → types → DFA lexer → parse tables → tree → query → cursor |
| The Layout Trail | beginner-jhj/mini_browser | C++ | 8 | 7 | Browser: QApplication → tokenizer → DOM → CSS parser → CSSOM → style → layout → paint |
| The Checkout Trail | yannick-cw/korb | Haskell | 8 | 7 | Grocery CLI: optparse → store selection → PKCE OAuth → HTTP client → search → basket → checkout |
| The Terminal Trail | manaflow-ai/cmux | Swift | 8 | 7 | Terminal: app delegate → GhosttyConfig → key dispatch → workspace → splits → GPU render → sockets |

Total: 23 trails in the index (13 existing + 10 new). Ready to commit when you'd like.

Commit and push this code, and if we're on a feature branch and there's not already a PR against dev, create one. If we're on the default (main or dev) branch, ask for confirmation before pushing.

After creating a PR (or if one already exists), run `/code-review:code-review` on it.

    - `composer.json` with `laravel/framework` → Laravel
+   - If none of the above match, the repo may use a non-standard or custom framework — use DeepWiki TOC and code exploration to identify the primary pipeline
 
-2. **Map pipeline stages**: For the detected framework, identify the real files that correspond to each trail stop:
+2. **Map pipeline stages**: For the detected framework (or custom architecture), identify the real files that correspond to each trail stop:

    - Find routing configuration

 | Laravel | Eloquent ORM, Blade Templates, Service Container, Artisan CLI |

+For repos that don't match a canonical framework, derive 4 party members from the project's core concepts/subsystems. Name them after the most important architectural components (e.g., for a TTS engine: Text Normalizer, Phoneme Encoder, Acoustic Model, Vocoder).

## Summary

## New Trails

| Trail | Repo | Language | Pipeline |
|-------|------|----------|----------|
| The Synthesis Trail | KittenML/KittenTTS | Python | TTS: model loading → preprocessing → phonemizer → ONNX inference → audio |
| The Production Trail | puemos/craftplan | Elixir | Phoenix/Ash: endpoint → router → auth → catalog → orders → forecasting |
| The Inference Trail | localgpt-app/localgpt | Rust | AI agent: HTTP gateway → memory → embedding search → prompt → providers → tools |
| The Relevance Trail | timescale/pg_textsearch | C | BM25: query parsing → planner hooks → tokenization → IDF → Block-Max WAND |
| The Packet Trail | moongate-community/moongatev2 | C# | UO server: bootstrap → TCP → Twofish encryption → middleware → game loop |
| The Rendering Trail | OlaProeis/Ferrite | Rust | Editor: rope buffer → comrak AST → blake3 cache → syntect → Mermaid → scroll |
| The Parser Trail | odvcencio/gotreesitter | Go | Parser: grammar registry → DFA lexer → parse tables → tree → query → cursor |
| The Layout Trail | beginner-jhj/mini_browser | C++ | Browser: tokenizer → DOM → CSS parser → CSSOM → style → layout → paint |
| The Checkout Trail | yannick-cw/korb | Haskell | Grocery CLI: optparse → PKCE OAuth → HTTP client → search → basket → checkout |
| The Terminal Trail | manaflow-ai/cmux | Swift | Terminal: app delegate → GhosttyConfig → key dispatch → workspace → GPU render |
